disclaimer: Educational assessment. CARSI is an IICRC-approved CEC provider; this is CARSI-original Australian training, not IICRC certification.
note: Australian-produced. Grounded in the course's own modules. Founder to review before wiring live.

quizzes.title: Introduction to Recovery of Submerged Items and Contents — Quiz

questions.questionText: Why does the course say securing flooded items quickly matters for their restorability?
options: Fast removal lets insurers approve the claim before the floodwater recedes.
Limiting how long items stay saturated before conservator evaluation raises restorability confidence, because degradation worsens over days of delay.
Items only begin degrading once they are fully dried, so speed prevents premature drying.
Quick removal removes the need for any contamination testing.

questions.questionText: How does the course recommend assessing the contamination that submerged items have been exposed to?
options: Submit initial floodwater samples for spectrographic contaminant analysis to guide neutralisation chemistry and post-treatment verification testing.
Rely on restorative drying alone, since drying removes toxic contaminants as the moisture leaves.
Grade contamination by visually inspecting the colour of the water.
Assume the floodwater is clean unless an odour is present.

questions.questionText: According to the course, how should new flood damage be distinguished from pre-existing aging degradation?
options: Assume all visible damage is flood-related to simplify the assessment.
Rely only on an item's weight to separate old damage from new damage.
Review historic collection manifests and compile photographic documentation, resin-fracture timelines and UV-exposure evidence to set a damage baseline.
Treat any damage that is not currently visible as pre-existing.

questions.questionText: What does the course describe for maintaining chain of custody during recovery?
options: A single supervisor memorises who handled each item.
Custody is recorded once, only when items reach the restoration facility.
Handlers are not tracked because the items are sealed inside bins.
Rotated handlers sign immutable digital custodial records that timestamp who takes responsibility at each loading and unloading junction.

questions.questionText: How should recovered collections be protected during transport to a processing facility?
options: Seal them in consolidated, manifest-labelled, continually desiccated polyethylene bins within temperature-regulated encapsulation.
Air-dry them in open trays during the drive to speed up drying.
Transport them loose so handlers can inspect them enroute.
Leave them in the original floodwater to keep them stable in transit.

questions.questionText: What is the course's guidance for creating drying conditions for preserved items?
options: Apply the highest safe heat to dry items as quickly as possible.
Adjust dehumidification set points to extract pore humidity gradually, without exceeding the materials' temperature tolerances.
Let items dry naturally in ambient air with no equipment.
Keep the surrounding humidity high to stop items from cracking.

questions.questionText: For which contents does the course say vacuum freeze-drying is suited?
options: Robust metal items that tolerate high heat.
Any item, because freeze-drying is always the fastest method.
Delicate contents such as soaked parchment scrolls, where direct heating would risk permanent distortion.
Only items that already have no remaining moisture.

questions.questionText: Why does the course recommend a gradual reintroduction of restored contents to normal conditions?
options: Rapid reintroduction to normal conditions locks in the restored state faster.
Items should return to service immediately after cleaning, with no trials.
Reintroduction is unnecessary once an item simply looks dry.
Slowly elevating humidity in isolated chambers lets contents reach equilibrium and avoids phase-change disruptions caused by sudden spikes.
